Valorization Pathways or Processing:
Wet or air granulation to form glassy slag, produces latent hydraulic reactivity [43]
Mechanical activation (i.e., grinding) to produce Supplementary Cementitious Material (SCM)  [38, 46]
Accelerated carbonation with carbon dioxide sequestration leading to stabilization of reactive Ca phases [38]
Thermal treatment for inducing crystallisation process for glass ceramics applications [43]
Crushing and screening for aggregate production [43]
Typical issues: Variability in phase ratio, potential leaching of trace elements [7]
Techno–Economic Aspects: Contributes to the construction and cement industries by reducing carbon dioxide emissions and producing a high volume, low cost material [6, 43]
End–use Applications: 75% used for hydraulic binder, aggregates, clinker raw materials, glass ceramics, bricks, silica gel and adsorbents [52, 43]
Associated Critical Raw Materials (CRMs): Sc, V, REEs – LREEs and HREEs (La, Y, Nd, Ce) [23]
Associated Strategic Raw Materials (SRMs: Mn, REEs for magnets ( Nd, Ce, Pr, Sm, Gd, Dy) [52]
Chemical Formulae:
Akermanite (Ca2MgSi2O7)
Monticellite (CaMgSiO4)
Gehlenite (Ca2Al2SiO7)​, (CaSO4SiO2)
Primarily amorphous phase after rapidly cooling process [6]
Elemental Composition: Ca, Si, Al, Mg, Fe, S, Mn, Ti, K, Na and trace of REEs (La, Ce, Nd, Pr, Y, Er,Dy) [13]
Physical State of Byproducts: Solid state: molten liquid during production stage forming granular or vitrified solid after air or water cooling [52]
pH Level: 10.0–11.0: highly alkaline [6]
Water Content: Negligible: solid oxide matrix after air or water cooling [52]

Byproduct Stream: Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF) or Linz–Donawitz (LD) slag
Process Stage of Generation: Steelmaking through Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF) or Linz–Donawitz (LD) processes [52, 38, 43]
Valorization Pathways or Processing:
Weathering (aging) for reduction of volume expansion and stabilization of free lime [23]
Metal recovery of iron (mainly) through magnetic separation [46]
Accelerated carbonation for immobilisation of Cr and V to improve durability [38, 17]
Crushing and grading for construction materials use [46]
Hot–stage modification during tapping to adjust basicity and add flux to control chemical composition [23]
Typical issues: High basicity, free lime expansion potential, potential leaching of Cr or V [38, 23]
Techno–Economic Aspects: Potential for carbon dioxide sequestration, internal recycling within the plant [23]
End–use Applications: Aggregates, asphalt and railway ballast, soil conditioning agent, cement clinker raw material [23, 43]
Chemical Formulae:
Dicalcium silicate (C2S)
Tricalcium silicate (C3S)
Srebrodolskite (Ca2Fe2O5)
Merwinite (Ca3Mg(SiO4)2)
Wüstite (FeO) [52]
Elemental Composition: Ca, Fe, Si, Mg, P, S, Mn, Al, Cr, K, V, Na [38]
Physical State of Byproducts: Solid state: stony, granular or rocky aggregate [23]
pH Level: 11.0–13.0: strongly alkaline with approximate average of 12.5 [6]
Water Content: Negligible: stony or rocky solid aggregates [23]

Byproduct Stream: Blast Furnace (BF) or Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF) sludge – wet dust
Process Stage of Generation: Off–gas cleaning system or water scrubbing during Blast furnace (BF) or Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF) operations [10, 7, 35]
Valorization Pathways or Processing:
Dewatering and pelletisation to improve handling and furnace recyclability [7]
Hydrometallurgical processes for removal of impurities like Zn and Pb prior to Fe recovery [8, 11]
Pyrometallurgical reduction process through Waelz or rotary kiln for volatilisation of Zn and upgradation of Fe fraction [44]
Magnetic separation for producing Fe–rich concentration, suitable for reuse [7]
Typical issues: Zn, Pb or oil contamination present, high moisture content, fine particle size distribution [7]
Techno–Economic Aspects: Zn and Pb recovery boosts the economic value and recyclability of raw material demand [7]
End–use Applications: Fe source for sinter, Zn and Pb recovery, magnetite concentrate [7]
Associated Critical Raw Materials (CRMs): P, REEs – LREEs and HREEs (La, Y) [7]
Elemental Composition: Fe, C, Zn, Pb, Ca, Mg, Si, S, Na, K, Mn [7]
Physical State of Byproducts: Wet solid or filter cake: slurry prior to dewatering [7]
Production Content:
BF: 2–22 kg/t Hot Metal (HM) (average of 12 kg/t)
BOF: 15–16 kg/t Liquid Steel (LS) [38]
pH Level: Alkaline: high moisture with high buffering capacity due to  9.0–10.6% of CaO content [10]
Water Content: High: wet gas–cleaning product handled as wet slurry or filter cake) [7]

Process Stage of Generation: Surface oxidation during hot rolling of steel [43, 7]

Magnetic upgrading to separate high–grade Fe oxide fractions from gangue [7]
Typical issues: Oil contamination hence requires de–oiling process [43]
Techno–Economic Aspects: High–grade Fe obtained, making it an economic driver for reuse [43]
End–use Applications: Feed for sinter or pellets, Fe powder, anti–corrosion pigments [43, 5]

Total Fe (70–95%)
SiO2 (generally <10%)
FeO (wüstite) primary phase [43]
Elemental Composition: Fe, Si, Mn, Co, Cr [5]
Physical State of Byproducts: Solid state: flaky particles or oiled scale [43]
pH Level: Near–neutral to alkaline [38]
Water Content: Low: flaky solid particles often containing surface oil [33]

Water Content: Low: managed as solid slag or fine flue dust [51]
